did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a region-based convolutional neural network (R-CNN) and convolutional neural networks (CNN) to automatically locate suspected keratinocytic skin cancer lesions on the face and predict their malignancy probability from clinical photographs.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is labeled as an 'Original Investigation' in JAMA Dermatology. It describes a diagnostic study where a region-based convolutional neural network was developed, trained on clinical photographs, and validated against a dataset of 673 patients and compared with dermatologists and the general public. It presents new experimental results, including AUC, F1 scores, and Youden index scores, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Keratinocytic Skin Cancer Detection on the Face Using Region-Based Convolutional Neural Network
brief_summary: This study evaluated a region-based convolutional neural network (R-CNN) and convolutional neural network (CNN) algorithm for automatically detecting keratinocytic skin cancer on the face using unprocessed clinical photographs. The algorithm was trained on 1,106,886 image crops derived from various sources, including the MED-NODE and Seven-Point Checklist datasets, and validated against a dataset of 673 patients from three hospitals. The primary evaluation metrics included the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), F1 score, and Youden index score. The algorithm achieved an AUC of 0.910 on the validation set and demonstrated an F1 score of 0.831 and Youden index of 0.675 at high-sensitivity thresholds. These performance metrics were comparable to those of 13 board-certified dermatologists and significantly higher than those of nondermatologic physicians. The results suggest that the algorithm can localize and diagnose facial skin cancer without preselection of suspicious lesions by dermatologists.

all_task_types.target: Location of suspected keratinocytic skin cancer lesions (bounding boxes) and malignancy probability.
all_task_types.details: The study uses a region-based convolutional neural network (R-CNN) to automatically locate (detect) suspected areas of skin cancer on the face. The algorithm generates bounding boxes for lesions and predicts a malignancy output (probability) to classify them as malignant or benign. The primary evaluation metrics include Average Precision (AP) for detection and AUC/F1 score for the classification of malignancy.

all_task_types.target: Malignancy status (malignant vs. benign/normal) of detected lesions.
all_task_types.details: The disease classifier predicts the probability of specific disorders (e.g., basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma) and a combined malignancy output. The task is binary/multiclass classification of lesion types and malignancy, evaluated via AUC, sensitivity, specificity, F1 score, and Youden index.

datasets_used.name: Primary Training Data Set
datasets_used.description: Clinical photographs from Asan Medical Center (collected 2003-2016), the MED-NODE data set, the Seven-Point Checklist Dermatology data set, and images from search engines.

use.training: Used as the primary training data set. Lesions of common benign disorders and normal skin structures were cropped and annotated to create the secondary training data set.
use.other: Used to create the secondary training data set via cropping and annotation.

datasets_used.name: Secondary Training Data Set
datasets_used.description: Derived from the primary training data set by cropping and manually annotating lesions of common benign disorders (nevus, lentigo, acne, seborrheic keratosis) and normal skin structures.

use.training: Used for training the algorithm with manually annotated benign and normal skin lesions.

datasets_used.name: Tertiary Training Data Set
datasets_used.description: Created by fully automatically annotating data using temporal models trained on the primary and secondary training data sets.
classes_used: Various benign and malignant lesions (inferred from training sources)

use.training: Used as part of the final training data set.

datasets_used.name: Final Training Data Set
datasets_used.description: The combination of the primary, secondary, and tertiary training data sets, comprising 1,106,886 image crops.

use.training: Used to train the blob detector (using faster-RCNN), fine image selector, and disease classifier (using CNNs: SENet, SE-ResNeXt-50, SE-ResNet-50).

datasets_used.name: DER Validation Data Set
datasets_used.description: Validation data set obtained from the Asan Medical Center Department of Dermatology. Comprises benign and malignant tumors. Images taken from outpatient rooms, photography studios, and treatment rooms with varied composition, background, and lighting.

use.validation: Used to validate the model. Images taken after 2018 to prevent data overlap with training. Pathologic diagnosis confirmed 81 patients with 3 malignant tumorous disorders, 251 with 6 benign tumorous disorders, and 54 with other benign disorders.

datasets_used.name: PS Validation Data Set
datasets_used.description: Validation data set obtained from the plastic surgery departments of Hallym University and Chonnam National University. Comprises malignant nodules and healthy individuals. Images taken in a photography studio with unified composition, constant background, and lighting.

use.validation: Used to validate the model. Two dermatologists confirmed location and diagnosis. Hallym images acquired Jan 2014-Sep 2018; Chonnam images acquired Jan 2010-Dec 2017.

datasets_used.name: Combined Validation Data Set
datasets_used.description: The combination of the DER and PS validation data sets, comprising 2844 images from 673 patients.

use.validation: Used to calculate the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.910.

datasets_used.name: Test Partition
datasets_used.description: A separate test set used for comparison with human participants (dermatologists, residents, nondermatologic physicians, and general public).

use.testing: Used to compare the algorithm's performance against 13 board-certified dermatologists, 34 dermatology residents, 20 nondermatologic physicians, and 52 members of the general public.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the algorithm and participants in distinguishing malignant from benign/normal lesions and determining biopsy necessity.

statistical_methods_used.method: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantify the overall diagnostic performance of the algorithm and compare it with dermatologists, residents, nondermatologic physicians, and the general public.
To compare the performance of the algorithm with participants at high-sensitivity thresholds.
To compare the performance of the algorithm with participants at high-sensitivity thresholds.
To evaluate the detection performance of the algorithm using intersection over union (IoU) thresholds.
To compare the F1 score and Youden index score of the algorithm with those of the human participants.
To determine statistical significance for the comparison of the algorithm with participants.
To calculate the area under the ROC curve (AUC).
To calculate the average precision (AP) score for detection.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference between the F1 score or Youden index score of the algorithm and those of the human participants (dermatologists, residents, nondermatologic physicians, or general public).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference between the F1 score or Youden index score of the algorithm and those of the human participants.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Findings were considered significant at P < .05. The algorithm showed significantly higher F1 and Youden index scores than nondermatologic physicians (P < .001) and significantly higher F1 scores than dermatology residents (P = .04). The algorithm's performance was comparable to that of dermatologists.

DER Validation Data Set.notes: The DER data set comprised 1570 images from 386 patients. The algorithm's performance was evaluated on this subset.

PS Validation Data Set.notes: The PS data set comprised 1274 images from 287 patients. The algorithm's performance was evaluated on this subset.

Combined Validation Data Set (DER + PS).notes: The combined validation data set comprised 2844 images from 673 patients. The algorithm achieved an AUC of 0.910.

Test Partition.notes: The test partition comprised 325 images from 80 patients. The algorithm's F1 score (0.831) and Youden index score (0.675) were significantly higher than those of nondermatologic physicians (P < .001) and comparable to dermatologists. The algorithm's F1 score was significantly higher than that of dermatology residents (P = .04). The general public had a sensitivity of 50.1%.

DER Data Set (Test Subset for ROC Compar.notes: This refers to a specific subset of 170 images from 40 patients used for ROC curve comparison in Figure 3C.

PS Data Set (Test Subset for ROC Compari.notes: This refers to a specific subset of 155 images from 40 patients used for ROC curve comparison in Figure 3E.

architecture_used.main_model: Region-based convolutional neural network (R-CNN) combined with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)

architecture_details.malignancy_output_formula: Sum of outputs for basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma in situ, keratoacanthoma, and malignant melanoma, plus 0.2 times the sum of outputs for actinic keratosis and ulcer.

training_data_sources: Primary training data set (clinical photographs from Asan Medical Center, MED-NODE, Seven-Point Checklist Dermatology, search engines)
Secondary training data set (cropped and manually annotated benign lesions and normal skin structures)
Tertiary training data set (automatically annotated using temporal models)

classification_or_output_head.details: The disease classifier predicts probabilities for 178 disorders. A malignancy output score is calculated by aggregating specific class outputs (malignant and high-risk benign lesions) to determine the final malignancy probability.

preprocessing_techniques: Image cropping: Lesions of common benign disorders and normal skin structures were cropped from the primary training data set to create the secondary training data set.
Manual annotation: Lesions in the secondary training data set were manually annotated based on image findings.
Automatic annotation: The tertiary training data set was annotated fully automatically using temporal models trained on the primary and secondary training data sets.
Quality control/Filtering: The fine image selector excluded inadequate image blobs and general object blobs.
Blob detection: A blob detector (faster-RCNN) was used to detect possible lesions of interest (blobs) from unprocessed clinical images.
conclusions: The region-based convolutional neural network algorithm can automatically localize suspected keratinocytic skin cancer lesions and predict their malignancy probability from unprocessed clinical photographs without preselection by dermatologists.
The algorithm achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.910 on the validation data set comprising 2844 images from 673 patients.
At a high-sensitivity threshold (T80), the algorithm demonstrated a sensitivity of 76.8% and a specificity of 90.6%.
The algorithm's diagnostic performance, measured by F1 score (0.831) and Youden index score (0.675), was comparable to that of 13 board-certified dermatologists (F1 score 0.835, Youden index 0.671).
The algorithm significantly outperformed nondermatologic physicians, with a higher F1 score (0.831 vs 0.653, P < .001) and Youden index score (0.675 vs 0.417, P < .001).
The algorithm's F1 score was significantly higher than that of dermatology residents (0.831 vs 0.815, P = .04).
The general public had a low sensitivity of 50.1% in identifying skin cancer, indicating that half of malignant lesions could be missed without professional evaluation.

The study suggests that deep learning algorithms trained on diverse, unprocessed clinical images can overcome challenges posed by facial anatomic structures and shades, offering a scalable solution for early skin cancer detection.
important_remarks: The study population is limited to an Asian cohort, which may affect generalizability to other ethnicities.
The validation dataset lacked biopsy-proven malignant melanoma cases, limiting the evaluation of melanoma detection capabilities.
The algorithm was trained on diverse, unprocessed clinical photographs from multiple sources (including search engines) to mitigate selection bias common in hospital archive-based studies.
Potential for high false-positive rates in real-world deployment is noted due to the inclusion of various benign lesions, normal skin structures, and general objects in the training data.
The study addresses the challenge of facial anatomic structures (curves, shades, nose, ears) that can confuse algorithms, demonstrating robustness in this specific context.
Data leakage was mitigated by selecting validation images taken after 2018, ensuring no overlap with the training data collected up to that point.

The algorithm's performance was comparable to board-certified dermatologists but significantly superior to nondermatologic physicians and dermatology residents in terms of F1 score and Youden index at high-sensitivity thresholds.
The general public had a low sensitivity (50.1%) in identifying skin cancer, highlighting the need for professional or AI-assisted evaluation.
